coredata.dc:title: Common materials, extraordinary behavior: An ultrasensitive and enantioselective strategy for D-Tryptophan recognition based on electrochemical Au@p-L-cysteine chiral interface
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Yanan
Zhang, Zihao
Pang, Yuanhao
Zhou, Xueying
Wang, Yu
Zhang, Yun
Yuan, Yali
coredata.dc:description: The poly-L-cysteine modified Au nanoparticles (Au@p-L-Cys) were constructed on electrode surface as a highly efficient chiral interface for tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers recognition via one step electropolymerization. With the aid of Cu2+, L-Cys residues and D-Trp target formed a sandwich complex D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys, while L-Trp was unable to form such complex due to the steric hindrance provided by the chiral interface, which was confirmed by the electrochemical and SEM results. With the introduction of ferricyanide probe, D-Trp produced significant current decrease while L-Trp produced a slight current increase, which implied the successful enantioselective recognition of Trp enantiomers (specifically D-Trp) in the true sense. This novel sensor showed a surprisingly wide linear range toward D-Trp of 6 × 10−7 M to 1 × 10−2 M, with a detection limit as low as 75 nM (S/N = 3). Moreover, the exclusive enantioselectivity toward D-Trp was discovered since other amino acids showed negligible interference to detection of D-Trp. The recovery of D-Trp in human serum was between 91.30 and 109.3%, which further verified the satisfying specificity and practicality of the proposed strategy. The coordination thermodynamics by UV–Vis spectroscopy and DFT simulation were also used to investigate the enantioselective mechanism. These results highlight the great potential of using Au@p-L-Cys to construct chiral interface for enantiomers recognition and hold the promise of practical application of electrochemical chiral sensors in fields like pharmaceutics and bioanalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enantioselective recognition
Electrochemical chiral interface
Cu2+-assisted complex
Tryptophan enantiomers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the obtained chiral sensor
Comparison of recognition interfaces with different modifications
Analytical performance of the chiral sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Guangxi Natural Science Foundation ( 2020GXNSFBA297116 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) CV curves and (B) EIS spectra recorded on different electrodes in solution containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]4-/3-: (a) bare GCE, (b) Au@p-L-Cys/GCE, (c) D-Trp-Cu2+-Au@p-L-Cys/GCE, and (d) L-Trp-Cu2+-Au@p-L-Cys/GCE.
SEM images of (A) Au@p-L-Cys/GCE, (B) Au@p-L-Cys/GCE immersed in 10 mM L-Trp, and (C) Au@p-L-Cys/GCE immersed in 10 mM D-Trp.
DPV curves measured in blank solution, 10 mM L-Trp, and 10 mM D-Trp at different modified electrodes: (A) Au/GCE, (B) p-L-Cys/GCE, and (C) Au@p-L-Cys/GCE.

Optimization of the experimental conditions for recognition of D-Trp at Au@p-L-Cys/GCE: (A) cycle numbers of CV scanning for electropolymerization, (B) coordination time, and (C) solution pH. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three repetitive analysis. All the subsequent error bars are calculated following the same process.

(A) DPV curves on Au@p-L-Cys/GCE in a series of D-Trp samples with different concentrations. (B) The linear relationship between the peak current change (ΔI) and the logarithm of D-Trp concentration (lgCD-Trp).
Response of Au@p-L-Cys/GCE to D-Trp at (A) four different electrodes and (B) various storage time.

DPV curves of 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]4-/3- measured on Au@p-L-Cys/GCE for enantiomers of different amino acids (10 mM each): (A) Trp, (B) Ala, (C) Tyr, (D) Pro, and (E) His. (F) ΔI values of the DPV responses shown in (A–E).
UV spectra of 0.15 mM L-Trp (A) and D-Trp (C) after mixing with L-Cys at various concentrations. Benesi-Hildebrand plots of L-Trp (B) and D-Trp (D) with L-Cys (pH = 7).
Molecular structures of the ground state simulated by the hybrid DFT method for (A) L-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys and (B) D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys complexes.
(A) DPV curves on Au@p-L-Cys/GCE in Trp racemic mixture with different D-Trp% (10–100%). (B) Linear relationship between ΔI and the D-Trp% in the racemic mixtures.
Illustration of chiral interface fabrication and enantiomer recognition.
Comparison of the proposed electrochemical sensor with other methods reported for the chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers.
Determination of D-Trp in human serum.

head.ce:title: Common materials, extraordinary behavior: An ultrasensitive and enantioselective strategy for D-Tryptophan recognition based on electrochemical Au@p-L-cysteine chiral interface

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials, Guilin University of Technology, 12 Jiangan Road, Guilin, 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials, Guilin University of Technology, 12 Jiangan Road, Guilin 541004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, 12 Jiangan Road, Guilin, 541004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, 12 Jiangan Road, Guilin 541004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials, Guilin University of Technology, 12 Jiangan Road, Guilin, 541004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The poly-L-cysteine modified Au nanoparticles (Au@p-L-Cys) were constructed on electrode surface as a highly efficient chiral interface for tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers recognition via one step electropolymerization. With the aid of Cu2+, L-Cys residues and D-Trp target formed a sandwich complex D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys, while L-Trp was unable to form such complex due to the steric hindrance provided by the chiral interface, which was confirmed by the electrochemical and SEM results. With the introduction of ferricyanide probe, D-Trp produced significant current decrease while L-Trp produced a slight current increase, which implied the successful enantioselective recognition of Trp enantiomers (specifically D-Trp) in the true sense. This novel sensor showed a surprisingly wide linear range toward D-Trp of 6 × 10−7 M to 1 × 10−2 M, with a detection limit as low as 75 nM (S/N = 3). Moreover, the exclusive enantioselectivity toward D-Trp was discovered since other amino acids showed negligible interference to detection of D-Trp. The recovery of D-Trp in human serum was between 91.30 and 109.3%, which further verified the satisfying specificity and practicality of the proposed strategy. The coordination thermodynamics by UV–Vis spectroscopy and DFT simulation were also used to investigate the enantioselective mechanism. These results highlight the great potential of using Au@p-L-Cys to construct chiral interface for enantiomers recognition and hold the promise of practical application of electrochemical chiral sensors in fields like pharmaceutics and bioanalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is one of the intrinsic features in nature world and chiral molecules are closely associated to our daily life. About 30% of pesticides, more than half of clinical medicines, and most biomolecules (amino acids, nucleic acid, enzyme, etc.) in living systems are chiral [1]. Generally, enantiomers of one compound possess identical physicochemical properties while they exhibit vastly different or even opposite bioactivity or pharmacological activity [2]. For instance, l-tryptophan (L-Trp), a basic structural unit of protein in living organisms, is widely involved in various life processes such as metabolism, immune responses, neuroendocrine, etc [3–5]. On the contrary, D-Trp is a non-protein amino acid and it cannot be metabolized efficiently or even lead to untoward effects on living systems [6–8]. Thus, efficient recognition and detection of chiral isomers is of great significance in fields including medical science, pharmaceutics, and biochemistry.

The existing techniques available for achieving the goal of chiral recognition mainly include nuclear magnetic resonance [9,10], capillary electrophoresis [11,12], circular dichroism spectroscopy [13,14], high performance liquid chromatography [15,16], and surface enhanced Raman scattering [17,18]. These traditional methods can offer certain detection specificity and accuracy, but most of them still suffer from time-consuming sample pretreatment, high-cost reagents, and complex professional operation. In order to address these issues, electrochemical method has attracted rising attention as competitive alternative due to its outstanding advantages, such as simple operation, high detection efficiency, and easy miniaturization for point-of-care testing [19–22].

For enantiomer recognition by electrochemical sensor, the crucial step is to construct a chiral interface containing efficient chiral selectors, with which enantiomers form different binding forces to produce different electrochemical signals [23]. Basically, the chiral interface are usually composed of various nanomaterials [24–26] and chiral components [27,28] to express the chiral selection function. Up to now, a series of intrinsic chiral nanomaterials [29,30] have been studied to build electrochemical chiral sensing platforms in addition to other types of materials like polysaccharides [31,32], chiral metal-organic frameworks [33,34], and amino acids and their derivatives [35,36]. These materials provide various chiral selectors to interact with enantiomer and translate the chiral molecular recognition behavior into discernible changes in electrochemical signals [37]. As small chiral ligands, amino acids are applied widely as chirality source in synthesis of various chiral nanomaterials [38] and construction of chiral interface [39]. Especially when cysteine (Cys) is used, Au–S bond renders the introduction of Au nanomaterials and brings extra function like signal amplification [40–42].

In most cases, electrochemical discrimination of enantiomers relies on the oxidation/reduction of the chiral molecules [43,44], which cause differences in the peak current intensities. However, enantiomers of low concentrations would usually produce low currents, leading to insufficient recognition of the enantiomers. Therefore, these chiral sensors measuring with peak current often show high detection limit (typically at mM level) and narrow linear range (often with 1–2 orders of magnitude) [45,46]. Besides, this type of electrochemical sensors relies on the intrinsic electroactivity of the chiral analytes [45], making it difficult for the analysis of non-electroactive chiral isomers. Nevertheless, it is noted that the interaction between the enantiomers and the chiral selectors is usually accompanied by corresponding changes in the resistance at the electrochemical interface. Thus, with the introduction of a proper electroactive probe, this resistance information can be sensitively translated into the electrochemical signal of probe [47]. Therefore, this method might be a suitable tool used for sensitive chiral discrimination without the requirements of electroactivity.

Herein, a novel electrochemical chiral interface for ultrasensitive recognition of D-Trp enantiomer was constructed by one-step modification. Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) were electrodeposited on the electrode surface together with chiral ligand L-Cys (Au@p-L-Cys), providing a chiral micro-environment for enantiomer recognition (as illustrated in Scheme 1 ). Upon the introduction of Trp enantiomers, the Cu2+ ion could mediate the specific capture of D-Trp through the sandwich complex formation of D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys, which gave rise to the nonconductive barrier and in turn impaired the electron transfer of the interface. Conversely, L-Trp has major difficulty to form such complex due to the steric hindrance effect on the chiral interface. With the aid of ferricyanide probe, the resistance information of the complex formation can be sensitively reflected by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) signal of probe, thus achieving the discrimination and quantitative detection of Trp enantiomers. To the best of our knowledge, this method might be one of the most sensitive electrochemical chiral sensors proposed for the analysis of Trp enantiomers with rather wide linear range and admirable enantioselectivity.

D-tryptophan, l-tryptophan, D-alanine, L-alanine, D-tyrosine, L-tyrosine, D-histidine, L-histidine, D-proline, L-proline, chloroauric acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), L-cysteine hydrochloride (99%), concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), and potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6], 99.5%) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.,. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) with various pH values were prepared using 0.1 M Na2HPO4 and 0.1 M KH2PO4. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

DPV and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660D, Shanghai Chenhua Instruments, China). The potential range, step potential and amplitude for the DPV were −0.3 V–0.6 V, 2 mV and 50 mV, respectively. The potential range for the CV was −0.2 V–0.6 V at a san rate of 0.1 V/s. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed with an electrochemical workstation (Autolab PGSTAT128N, Metrohm Co., Ltd) in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz–1 M Hz. The EIS data was simulated with the Zview software. These electrochemical measurements were performed at room temperature in a conventional three-electrode system with a saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode, and a modified glassy carbon electrode (3 mm in diameter) as the working electrode. The involved electrolyte was 1 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7) containing 1 M KCl.

The morphology of modified electrodes was characterized on an SU 5000 field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Hitachi, Japan), and the UV–Vis spectra of different samples were conducted by using a TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Beijing, China).

Prior to surface modification, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was carefully polished with 0.3 μm and 0.05 μm Al2O3 powder, followed by a thorough rinse with distilled water with the aid of ultrasound. Then, the clean GCE was placed into a mixture consisting of 0.5 M H2SO4, 0.6 mM HAuCl4 and 1 mM L-Cys and CV technique (30 cycles, −0.5–1.5 V, 0.1 V/s) was applied for the electropolymerization to get Au@p-L-Cys modified GCE (Au@p-L-Cys/GCE). According to the same process, the Au NPs modified GCE (Au/GCE) and the poly-L-Cys modified GCE (p-L-Cys/GCE) were prepared in the mixture of 0.5 M H2SO4 and 0.6 mM HAuCl4, and the mixture of 0.5 M H2SO4 and 1 mM L-Cys, respectively.

The chiral recognition of the Trp enantiomers was conducted by the DPV measurement. The as-prepared Au@p-L-Cys/GCE was first incubated in a mixture of Trp enantiomers at varied concentration and 0.1 mM Cu(NO3)2, and then transferred to the electrolyte of 1 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7) for DPV measurement. The DPV peak current was adopted as the detection signal. The ΔI value for each sample was defined as: ΔI = Iblank -ITrp, where ITrp and Iblank were obtained from DPV results of the solution with and without Trp sample, respectively. Specificity experiments were performed according to the same procedures, but adding other types of amino acids instead of D-Trp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the obtained chiral sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The stepwise construction of the sensing interface of Au@p-L-Cys/GCE was studied by both CV and EIS. As shown in Fig. 1 A, a pair of well-defined reversible redox peaks of Fe(CN)6 3−/4- are observed on the bare GCE (curve a). After the electrodeposition of Au@p-L-Cys, the peak current increases a little accompanied by slight increase of peak potential difference (curve b), due to the excellent conductivity of Au NPs offset by nonconductive p-L-Cys. When incubated with D-Trp and Cu2+, the electron transfer performance of the modified electrode sharply decreased and the pair of the redox peaks almost disappeared completely (curve c), implying the capture of abundant D-Trp onto the electrode surface through Cu2+-assisted complexation. Interestingly, however, after the introduction of L-Trp, the CV behavior (curve d) was nearly the same with that of modified electrode (curve b), indicating that L-Trp was difficult to be trapped onto the chiral interface due to the strong steric hindrance in the same condition. This CV result preliminarily demonstrated the successful construction of the Au@p-L-Cys chiral interface and its expected chiral recognition ability toward D-Trp analyte.

EIS is another effective and convenient tool to probe the electron transfer procedure at the electrode surface. In Fig. 1B, the EIS spectra of different modified electrodes obtained in the frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 1 M Hz were represented by Nyquist plots, in which the semicircle part in the higher frequency region denoted the limited process of charge transfer and the linear part in the lower frequency region showed the diffusion-controlled process (Zw). When negligible charge transfer resistance (Rct) was recorded for the bare GCE (101.6 Ω), a limited increase of Rct (672.1 Ω) was found after the electropolymerization of Au@p-L-Cys on GCE surface (curve b). The introduction of D-Trp brought a significant increase of Rct to 7492 Ω (curve c), while L-Trp only induced a far less Rct (1368 Ω, curve d). These data agreed with the aforementioned CV result and further confirmed the enantioselectivity of the obtained electrochemical sensor based on Au@p-L-Cys/GCE. Meanwhile, the obvious Rct difference upon target binding made it possible to achieve chiral detection by applying an electroactive probe.

SEM was applied to further investigate the interface state of the Au@p-L-Cys/GCE before and after its immersion in Trp solution. Fig. 2 A displayed the sensing interface of Au@p-L-Cys/GCE, which possessed nanoparticles (Au@p-L-Cys) uniformly distributed on GCE. After incubation in L-Trp, the surface condition changed slightly (Fig. 2B), indicating very few L-Trp interacted with Au@p-L-Cys. However, after the recognition of the D-Trp, as shown in Fig. 2C, a significant morphology change was observed that a dense layer was formed on electrode surface. This was consistent with the electrochemical result in Fig. 1 that abundant Cu2+-mediated complex formed on the Au@p-L-Cys/GCE, demonstrating the enantioselectivity of the constructed chiral interface toward D-Trp as well.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of recognition interfaces with different modifications
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrochemical behaviors of Trp enantiomers on electrodes through different modification procedures were comparatively investigated by DPV technique under the same condition. Fig. 3 A depicted clearly that when modified only with Au NPs (Au/GCE), the peak currents measured from the blank, D-Trp and L-Trp sample solution almost overlapped, implying that the Au/GCE was not able to recognize the Trp enantiomers due to the lack of chiral sites. In the case of modification only with L-Cys (p-L-Cys/GCE), a small peak current difference between D-Trp and L-Trp was observed (Fig. 3B). Taking the peak current of blank solution as baseline, it was interesting to find that with the introduction of D-Trp, the peak current decreased a little as expected, while L-Trp induced a slight but clear increase of peak current, i.e., ΔI
D (Iblank - ID) was positive and ΔI
L (Iblank – IL) was negative. This phenomenon was further magnified when Au NPs and L-Cys were both modified on GCE (Au@p-L-Cys/GCE). As displayed in Fig. 3C, the peak current of D-Trp dropped dramatically, together with a very little rise for the peak current of L-Trp.

Based on the comparison result, several conclusions could be made. First, based on the contrary result of ΔI
D and ΔI
L (positive for ΔI
D and negative for ΔI
L ) in Fig. 3C, it strongly signaled that the truly enantioselective recognition of Trp isomers could be successfully achieved on Au@p-L-Cys/GCE, which was, to the best of our knowledge, rarely reported before. Also, the sensitive response to D-Trp gave rise to the quantitative detection of D-Trp. Second, from Fig. 3A and B, the obtained result indicated that Au NPs could not provide chiral sites, and L-Cys rendered chiral selectors on the interface, yet the recognition efficiency and sensitivity were not satisfactory. Only when both of them, i.e. Au@p-L-Cys, were electropolymerized on electrode, the synergy effect between Au NPs and L-Cys made the chiral recognition performance greatly improved. Besides, the large specific surface area of Au@p-L-Cys/GCE might play a role in there as well.

For the determination of D-Trp, the modified electrode obtained through electropolymerization was first immersed in D-Trp sample solution to ensure sufficient coordination of Trp on the electrode surface to form sandwich complex D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys, and then DPV technique was applied to obtain the corresponding current signal of redox probe Fe(CN)6 4−/3-. Therefore, the conditions which might influence the procedure, such as coordination time, solution pH, and electropolymerization conditions, were studied to achieve the optimum detection performance.

During the optimization, the modified electrode was adopted to obtain current signal after incubation in D-Trp sample and the blank sample under different conditions; ΔI
D (Iblank - ID) was used as the comparison standard. The cycle number of CV scanning for the electropolymerization showed a key influence on the thickness and surface roughness of Au@p-L-Cys sensing interface, which had a great impact on the sandwich complex formation. It was found in Fig. 4 A that ΔI
D obtained under different CV cycles reached peak value at the optimal cycle number of 30. Fewer CV cycles led to the formation of incomplete and rather few Au@p-L-Cys with insufficient chiral cites, while excess number of CV cycles made the electrode modifier with large resistance for electron transferring and disadvantageous surface condition for the D-Trp binding.

To examine the effect of the D-Trp coordination time on the recognition efficiency, Au@p-L-Cys/GCE was incubated in the sample solution with Cu2+ for 5, 10, 20, 30, 60 and 90 min, respectively. Fig. 4B displayed that the corresponding ΔI
D value gradually increased as the incubation time extended from 10 to 60 min, since the prolonged reaction time would benefit the recognition and capture of more D-Trp analyte on the sensing interface. However, too long incubation time resulted in the decrease of ΔI
D , probably ascribed to partial dissociation of D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys complex in solution. Thus, 60 min was chosen as the optimal coordination time for following experiments.

Fig. 4C indicated that the solution pH for D-Trp coordination displayed an obvious effect on the sensor response. The recognition and detection efficiency was highest at pH 7. Since the isoelectric point of Trp is around 6, D-Trp molecules are positively charged when pH is lower than 6. Therefore, the stronger electrostatic repulsion occurred between the positively charged Trp and the positively charged Cu2+ ions when pH getting lower, leading to poor complexation of D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys. Although D-Trp molecules were negatively charged when pH increased from 6, the hydrolysis of the Cu2+ ions happened at pH 8 and got more and more serious as pH getting higher, which in turn hampered the formation of D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys complex on the electrode interface. Consequently, pH 7 was recommended as the best acidity for the D-Trp incubation and detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the chiral sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions, the analytical performance of the proposed chiral sensor was evaluated in the detection of a series of artificial samples with varying D-Trp levels (0–10 mM). The recorded DPV responses were provided in Fig. 5 A. It was easily seen that the peak current at around +0.2 V decreased with the increase of D-Trp concentration, showing a negative D-Trp level-dependent electrochemical signal. Higher level of D-Trp enabled the formation of more nonconductive complex of D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys on the electrode interface, leading to stronger blockage to electron transfer and hence lower current response. Fig. 5B depicted the linear relationship between logarithm of D-Trp concentrations (logC D-Trp) and DPV peak current difference (ΔI), presenting a calibration curve ranging from 6.1 × 10−7 M to 1 × 10−2 M, a surprisingly wide linear range. The limit of detection (LOD) for D-Trp was estimated to be ∼75 nM (S/N = 3). In comparison with some recently-reported chiral sensors for the analysis of Trp enantiomers, the developed Au@p-L-Cys/GCE revealed comparable or even better performance in terms of preparation simplicity, detection selectivity, and LOD (Table 1 ). Our new sensor exhibited an extremely wide linear concentration range over 5 orders of magnitude with admirable sensitivity, and had no requirement to analyte electroactivity.

To evaluate the reproducibility of Au@p-L-Cys/GCE, four different electrodes modified via the same procedure were fabricated and used to obtain the current response under 10 mM D-Trp. Fig. 6 A implied that the current deviation of four electrodes was controlled within 2.9%, and the relative standard deviation in three repetitive assays of each electrode was less than ∼4.2%, further suggesting its satisfactory analytical reproducibility. For stability test, DPV determination were carried out in the interval of 12 days. The peak current response of Au@p-L-Cys/GCE remained above 90% of the initial signal after continuous determination every 3 days (Fig. 6B), indicating that Au@p-L-Cys/GCE had a good long-term stability.

In order to examine the selectivity of this chiral sensor toward D-Trp, 4 types of biological relevant amino acid enantiomers (10 mM each), including alanine (Ala), tyrosine (Tyr), proline (Pro), and histidine (His), were assayed under identical conditions to record the DPV result. As shown in Fig. 7 A, all of the DPV responses from other amino acids resembled the blank sample as expected (a little higher intensity), only with D-Trp as the exception. The obvious ΔI up to −36 μA was achieved for D-Trp sample, while much smaller ΔI value ranging from 1 to 7 μA were obtained from other 9 amino acids (Fig. 7B–E). The obvious difference in data indicated the outstanding recognition specificity and efficiency of the proposed chiral sensor toward the D-Trp analyte, demonstrating the satisfactory enantioselectivity of the constructed Au@p-L-Cys interface. The reason might be speculated that only D-Trp possessed a unique conformation that was suitable for the formation of Cu2+-mediated D-Trp/Cu2+/L-Cys complex. Such ternary complexation rarely took place in the case of L-Trp and other amino acids, due to the steric hindrance produced by the chiral interface. Moreover, the D-Trp molecule containing two rings was beneficial to form denser non-conductive layer on the electrode interface to present sensitive response.

To illustrate the chiral-recognition mechanism, the coordination thermodynamics were studied by UV–Vis spectroscopy of 0.15 mM Trp enantiomers in a series of L-Cys solutions with various concentrations in the presence of 0.5 mM Cu2+. As can be seen in Fig. 8 A and C, the absorbance of the Trp enantiomers gradually rose with the concentration increment of L-Cys, and the variation of D-Trp was higher than L-Trp, which was indicative of the stronger interactions between L-Cys and D-Trp. Furthermore, the complex constant and stoichiometric ratio were calculated according to the Benesi and Hildebrand method [55], the formula described as follows:
(1)
1
A −
A 0
=
1
Δ ε ×
[
T r p
]
+
1
Δ ε ×
[
T r p
]
× K ×
[
L − C y s
]
n
where A0 and A represent the absorbance of Trp enantiomers at 279 nm without and with the addition of varied L-Cys, respectively. Δε is the change of the extinction coefficient of Trp enantiomers caused by the addition of L-Cys. [Trp] and [L-Cys] stand for the concentration of Trp enantiomers and L-Cys, respectively. K is the complex constant and n is the stoichiometric ratio.

By fitting the relationship of 1/(A-A0) and 1/[L-Cys]n, a good linear relationship between 1/(A-A0) and 1/[L-Cys] was observed (Fig. 8B, D), i.e. n = 1. This indicated that molar ration between L-Cys and Trp enantiomers was 1:1, agreed with the ternary structure of Trp-Cu2+- L-Cys mentioned before [56]. Therefore, taking D-Trp as the example, the complexation of Trp on chiral interface in the presence of Cu2+ presumably went through the following process. In the mixture of Cu2+ and D-Trp, a transient complex of D-Trp-Cu2+-D-Trp could be formed [57]. After the immersion of Au@p-L-Cys/GCE into the mixture, the obvious current response decrease was observed (Fig. 3C), implying that L-Cys could exchange one D-Trp from D-Trp-Cu2+-D-Trp to form D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys [56].

The complex constant K for D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys and L-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys were calculated to be 1237.2 and 335.1, respectively. The significant difference of K suggested that L-Cys had a much higher binding capacity with D-Trp. Furthermore, the ground state structures of D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys and L-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys were analyzed by density functional theory (DFT) method at B3LYP/6-31G(d) level within the Gaussian 09 package (Fig. 9 ). Their energy difference was calculated to be −21.59 kJ mol−1. Having lower energy, D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys complex was more stable than L-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys, due to the steric hindrance of L-Trp and L-Cys during complex formation. In other words, it was more difficult for L-Cys to exchange one L-Trp to form L-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys. Overall, the calculation of complex constant and energy both provided the solid theoretical basis for the enantioselective affinity between D-Trp and L-Cys.

Whether the developed chiral sensor owns the ability to predict the ratio of Trp isomers in a racemic mixture is of great importance for practical applications. Here, such chiral recognition capability was studied through the ee value (the excess of one enantiomer to another) determination. Ten racemic mixtures with different ee ratios, from 10% to 100% D-Trp, were detected and the result was displayed in Fig. 10 . The corresponding DPV peak current decreased as the percentage of D-Trp increased. There is also a good linear relationship between the peak current difference and D-Trp percentage (ΔI = 1.29584 × 10−7lgCD-Trp + 2.12973 × 10−5 (R2 = 0.998)). The data suggested that this chiral sensor held the promise to predict the relative ratio of D-Trp in the enantiomer mixture.

To further verify the practicality of the proposed sensor, D-Trp concentration in human serum samples were determined, as well as serum samples spiked with standard D-Trp solution. The human serum was first centrifuged three times at 10,000 rpm for 15 min each time, and the supernatant was then diluted 1000 times with 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution at pH 7. As provided in Table 2 , it showed that no D-Trp was detected in serum samples and the recovery results were from 91.30 to 108.3% for the spiked samples. This acceptable result proved the relatively high accuracy and practicality of this novel chiral sensor for D-Trp detection in real complex matrices.

A highly effective enantioselective chiral interface, Au@p-L-Cys/GCE, was constructed by a simple one-step electropolymerization for Trp enantiomer recognition. With the assistance of Cu2+, D-Trp formed more stable D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys complex on the interface. By introducing an electroactive probe, D-Trp had a sharply decreased DPV response and L-Trp had a slightly increased response compared with the blank sample. The contrasting response of the proposed sensor toward D-Trp and L-Trp made the successful enantioselective recognition of Trp enantiomers in the true sense. The coordination thermodynamics by UV–Vis spectroscopy and DFT simulation further confirmed the D-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys complex with lower energy and higher stability than L-Trp-Cu2+-L-Cys, due to the strong steric hindrance provided by the chiral interface. A linear range up to 5 orders of magnitude and a detection limit as low as ∼75 nM was achieved for D-Trp, making it one of the most sensitive electrochemical chiral sensors for Trp enantiomers assay. Furthermore, the proposed chiral sensor showed admirable enantioselectivity toward D-Trp in presence of other amino acids and acceptable recovery in human serum samples. Thus, such a simple strategy may open up a new avenue for the chiral interface construction and electrochemical recognition of enantiomers in a wide range. Our future work will continue to focus on the admirable enantioselectivity toward D-Trp in this system by more advanced techniques to find out the key factor, which can effectively guide the design of chiral interface construction with suitable chiral recognition probe for numerous chiral targets in medical science, pharmaceutics and biochemistry.

Yanan Deng: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zihao Zhang: Data curation, Writing, Investigation. Yuanhao Pang: Data curation, Writing, Investigation. Xueying Zhou: Writing – review & editing. Yu Wang: Data curation. Yun Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Yali Yuan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2020GXNSFBA297116).
